file_name: 1. Lee & Lee. (2025). An Extended Dataset of Educational Quality Across Countries (1970–2023) 3.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using the harmonized cross-country test-score panel (1970–2023) anchored to the TIMSS 1995 metric, the study leveraged economic and education indicators to predict missing country-year test scores via LASSO regression, finding strong predictive performance (R² ≈ 0.91; RMSE ≈ 17.5 in-sample and ≈ 15.7 out-of-sample).

file_name: 10. Aydoğan & Tat. (2025). Investigating the Performance of Artificial Neural Networks in Predicting Affective Responses .pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 (Lebanon) Awacom item responses, the study leveraged Item1, Item2, Item4, Item5, and Item6 responses to predict Item3 responses with an ANN, finding moderate overlap between predicted and actual responses (match ratio ≈ 0.63; Kappa ≈ 0.42) and RMSE around 0.62, while validity/reliability analyses indicated that predicted items still supported the latent construct despite weakening some fit indices.

file_name: 100. Ulitzsch et al. (2021). Combining Clickstream Analyses And Graph-Modeled Data Clustering For Identifying Common Response Processes.pdf
canonical_variable: Dominant response processes (cliques of action patterns)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIAAC 2012 PSTRE (US sample) item U01a process data, the study leveraged action-pattern similarity (based on LCS overlap and action-level timing) and then applied cluster edge deletion to identify homogeneous cliques representing dominant response processes, finding that incorporating action-level times increased differentiation (more and smaller cliques) compared with action-only similarity.

file_name: 101. Tang et al. (2021). An exploratory analysis of the latent structure of process data via action sequence.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIAAC 2012 PSTRE process data, the study leveraged autoencoder-derived process features—capturing sequence length/attentiveness and interface-related behavior—to predict derived binary indicators (including item outcomes), finding that the extracted features achieved high out-of-sample prediction accuracy (all >0.80; 35 nearly perfect).

file_name: 102. Costa et al. (2021). Improving the Precision of Ability Estimates Using Time-On-Task Variables: Insights From the PISA 2012 Computer-Based Assessment of Mathematics.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics the study leveraged response accuracy and time-on-task information (modeled via hierarchical latent-variable speed factors) to estimate student math proficiency, finding that including response times increased measurement precision (higher EAP reliability and lower average standard errors) for nearly all countries, with the largest gain already achieved by the simple-structure hierarchical model (M2) and only modest additional improvement from cross-loadings (M3).

file_name: 103. Scherer et al. (2024). The potential of international large‑scale assessments for meta‑analyses in education.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using ILSA literature synthesis (systematic review; no single student-level analytic micro-dataset) the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This methodological review synthesizes four approaches for integrating ILSA data into education meta-analyses: separate meta-analyses, indirect inclusion via Bayesian meta-analysis, one-stage direct inclusion, and two-stage direct inclusion.

file_name: 104. Jung et al. (2022). Automated Scoring of Constructed- Response Items Using Artificial Neural Networks in International Large-scale Assessment.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2019 eTIMSS constructed-response item SE71054 the study leveraged human rater scoring and IRT-expected scoring (used to weight/filter training instances) to predict item correctness with an ANN, finding that automated scores matched human scores very closely (r≈0.93) and that IRT-based weighting/filtering did not materially change performance.

file_name: 105. Stiff et al. (2023). Research engagement in the Progress in International Reading Literacy Study: A systematic review.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIRLS the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Reading Literacy, finding that This paper is a systematic review that synthesizes how PIRLS has been used in peer-reviewed research, rather than training or evaluating machine learning models on PIRLS student data.

file_name: 106. Zhai et al. (2023). Machine learning investigation of optimal psychoemotional well-being factors for students’ reading literacy.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for B-S-J-Z (China) students, the study leveraged socioeconomic status (ESCS), learning goals, disciplinary climate, reading engagement stimulation, and parents’ emotional support to predict high vs low reading literacy, finding that the SVM model achieved high classification accuracy (≈0.905 with the optimal 15-feature set; 0.871 on the test set) and that ESCS was the most important feature according to SHAP.

file_name: 107. Tan et al. (2025). Using machine learning algorithms to predict students’ general self-efficacy in PISA 2018.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 the study leveraged mastery goal orientation, meaning of life, proud, positive emotions, and learning aspiration to predict students’ general self-efficacy (continuous IRT score), finding that XGBoost outperformed Lasso and achieved test-set R² ≈ 0.467 with low error (RMSE ≈ 9.70, MAE ≈ 7.22).

file_name: 108. Goldman et al. (2025). Artificial Intelligence to Support Writing Outcomes for Students With Disabilities.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using ILSA literature synthesis (systematic review; no single student-level analytic micro-dataset) the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that The article does not estimate ML models or report predictive performance metrics.

file_name: 109. Fink et al. (2024). Methodological aspects of the highly adaptive testing design for PISA.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 MST design the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This methods paper formalizes the highly adaptive testing (HAT) design for PISA as an operationally feasible extension of computerized adaptive testing with shadow testing.

file_name: 11. Demir & Karaboğa. (2021). Modeling mathematics achievement with deep learning methods.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Turkey the study leveraged Gender, sense of school belonging, socioeconomic status (ESCS), home possessions, and parents’ emotional support (plus ICT-related variables) to predict mathematics achievement class (successful vs unsuccessful), finding that the Jordan neural network achieved the highest test performance (Accuracy 0.826, AUC 0.739).

file_name: 110. Ang et al. (2020). Big Educational Data & Analytics: Survey, Architecture and Challenges.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: This paper provides a comprehensive survey of big educational data and analytics, covering data sources (e.g., LMS, MOOC, OER, social media, linked data), data collection/mining/database topics, big-data platforms/architectures (e.g., Hadoop, Spark, Samza), and analytics approaches (predictive analytics, learning analytics, recommendation systems, graph analytics, visual/immersive analytics).

file_name: 111. Bernardo et al. (2023). Profiling low-proficiency science students in the Philippines using machine learning.pdf
canonical_variable: Poor vs better science proficiency (Level ≤1b vs ≥1a)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Philippines science the study leveraged sense of belonging, perceived student cooperation, experienced bullying, work mastery motivation, and access to smartphones with internet at home to predict poor science proficiency (Level ≤1b vs ≥1a), finding that the Random Forest classifier achieved test accuracy of 0.74 and AUC of 0.83; SHAP was used to interpret the top 15 predictors, but the approach is predictive rather than causal and the manuscript does not report applying PISA sampling weights.

file_name: 112. Zhang et al. (2023). Accurate Assessment via Process Data.pdf
canonical_variable: PSTRE latent proficiency (process-incorporated vs response-based agreement with reference set)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIAAC 2012 PSTRE data from the scoring set and reference set split (7+7 items) across five countries/regions, the study leveraged process-derived features (embedded via MDS and combined through Rao–Blackwellization) together with response-based latent trait estimates to predict reference-set PSTRE proficiency, finding that process-incorporated scoring achieved consistently lower MSE and higher Kendall’s τ than response-based scoring, especially when the two estimators disagreed most.

file_name: 113. Tyack et al. (2024). Using convolutional neural networks to automatically score eight TIMSS 2019 graphical response items.pdf
canonical_variable: Automated CNN score classification vs human rater (dichotomous/partial credit as applicable)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2019 eTIMSS Grade 4 graphical response item the study leveraged human rater score category, item identity, grade level, content domain, and item complexity to predict the human rater classification from student images, finding that CNN-based automated scoring achieved near-perfect agreement (≈99%+ accuracy and F1 ≈0.995–0.999) for dichotomous items.

file_name: 114. Khoudi et al. (2024). Identifying the contextual factors related to the reading performance of Moroccan fourth-grade students from a Machine Learning-based Approach..pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIRLS 2016 Morocco Grade 4 Reading the study leveraged reading achievement too low, students’ reading confidence, early literacy tasks performance, student age, and reading attitudes to predict whether students were in the Low reading proficiency group versus Others, finding that Gradient Boosting achieved the highest classification performance (Accuracy 80.97%, F1 63.80%) and identified 12 key contextual variables overall.

file_name: 115. Rausch et al. (2024). Designing an International Large‑Scale Assessment of Professional Competencies and Employability Skills: Emerging Avenues and Challenges of OECD’s PISA‑VET.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using OECD the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This is a non-empirical methodology paper describing the design of OECD’s PISA-VET assessment framework rather than reporting predictive modeling results.

file_name: 116. Ye & Yuan. (2022). Using a machine learning approach to explore non-cognitive factors affecting reading, mathematics, and science literacy in china and the united states.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading the study leveraged perceived PISA test difficulty (PISADIFF), meta-cognition credibility (METASPAM), meta-cognition summarizing (METASUM), socioeconomic status (ESCS), and science learning time (SMINS) to predict reading achievement, finding that metacognitive awareness and students’ perceived difficulty were among the most influential predictors, with ESCS and school learning-time variables also contributing.

file_name: 117. Lim et al. (2024). Exploration of Variables Predicting Sense of School Belonging Using the Machine Learning Method—Group Mnet.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2015 (KOR) the study leveraged student gender, home ICT/educational resources, school climate (e.g., being threatened), teacher relationship indicators, and life satisfaction to predict students’ sense of school belonging, finding that positive peer/teacher relationships, parental support, cooperation tendencies, learning/physical activities, and life satisfaction were associated with higher belonging while anxiety, unfair teacher treatment, and being threatened by peers were associated with lower belonging; predictive performance was stable (RMSE≈0.39) but the manuscript notes that ML-selected predictors are not necessarily statistically significant in a conventional inferential sense.

file_name: 118. Rutkowski et al. (2024). The limits of inference: reassessing causality in international assessments.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: The authors contrast RCTs (gold standard) with quasi-experimental designs and use published examples to illustrate where causal claims may fail.

file_name: 119. Cortes et al. (2025). Evaluating uncertainty: the impact of the sampling and assessment design on statistical inference in the context of ILSA.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIRLS 2016 data to compute design effects for multip, the document summarizes reading achievement in relation to See outcome_summary (descriptive associations not coded as predictors), reporting that This paper is not an ML prediction/classification study; it develops and applies a design-effect framework to decompose design-based uncertainty in ILSA inference.

file_name: 12. Gomez-Talal et al. (2025). Interpretable Machine Learning Models for PISA Results in Mathematics.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics data for Spain, the study leveraged grade repetition, digital device ownership, extra mathematics class participation, number of books at home, and pre-class study/homework behavior to predict Low vs High mathematics proficiency, finding that the stacking meta-model achieved very high discrimination (AUC ≈ 0.9766) and that SHAP highlighted these factors as key drivers.

file_name: 120. Rebai et al. (2020). A graphically based machine learning approach to predict secondary schools performance in Tunisia.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2012 Tunisia school-level the study leveraged school size, class size, proportion of girls, competition, and parental pressure to predict school inefficiency (DDF/DEA) and found that inefficiency is most strongly associated with school characteristics—especially proportion of girls and school size—while competition and location show additional effects in linear robustness models; the authors also emphasize non-linear interactions captured by regression trees/random forests (with no causal claims beyond prediction).

file_name: 121. Braun et al. (2025). Rethinking TIMSS quality assurance: utilizing neural network models with regression‑based bias mitigation strategies for validating country‑level math and science achievement scores.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2011→2015 Grade 8 Mathematics the study leveraged socioeconomic, demographic, and student/teacher background variables to predict country-level mean math achievement and compute residuals, finding that most countries’ residuals were well-behaved while a small set of countries showed large positive residuals (top 20%) consistent with potential workflow anomalies.

file_name: 122. Schmitz & Sonnleitner (2025). Evaluating AI‑generated vs. human‑written reading comprehension passages: an expert SWOT analysis and comparative study for an educational large‑scale assessment.pdf
canonical_variable: Discernibility of authorship (human vs AI; % correct/definitely AI)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using Study 2 online blinded review the study leveraged text type, prompt design, and genre to predict authorship discernibility, finding that reviewers could not reliably distinguish human-written from GPT-4-generated texts (no category exceeded 50% correct), with informative zero-shot and narrative human-written texts showing the highest—but still limited—discernibility.

file_name: 123. Jewsbury & Johnson (2025). Principal component analysis on the covariance matrix for data reduction in large‑scale assessments.pdf
canonical_variable: Proficiency group mean estimates (PVs) bias and MSE
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using the simulation design based on 2019 NAEP Mathematics Grade 8 the study leveraged variance-weighted PCA-COV (and hybrid variants including main reporting-group dummies) to predict PV-based proficiency group means, finding that PCA-COV greatly reduces bias and MSE compared with PCA-COR, with PCA-COV-Hybrid achieving the lowest mean bias for small reporting groups.

file_name: 123. Schipper et al. (2025). Identifying students’ solution strategies in digital mathematics assessment using log data.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using CEDRE 2019 Grade 9 digital mathematics data for the “Product Equation” item, the study leveraged entered values (x and -2/3), interaction counts, and time-based log aggregates to identify five distinct solution-strategy classes, finding that students differed meaningfully in in-assessment behavior (e.g., absent, algebraic, pragmatic, trial-and-error, disengaged).

file_name: 124. Mirazchiyski & Gershteyn (2024). ﻿Technology factors related to the differences in paper and online reading scores in PIRLS 2016.pdf
canonical_variable: Mode difference in reading for information (e-reading PV minus paper PV)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIRLS 2016 Grade 4 Reading for information (paper vs.

canonical_variable: 288 schools) achievement | predicts mathematics achievement measured by pv1math–pv10math from gender | ESCS achievement | immigration status achievement | student behavior hindering learning achievement
policy_recommendation_excerpt: [Purely Methodological / No Explicit Policy Statement]

file_name: 126. He et al. (2024). The relationship between personal‑collaborative motivation profiles and students’ performance in collaborative problem solving.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2015 CPS data from China (B-S-J-G regions), the study leveraged motivation profile membership along with gender, ESCS, SOIAICT, and school factors (notably PROSTCE) to predict CPS achievement (PVs).

file_name: 127. Sun et al. (2023). A Theoretical Framework for a Mathematical Cognitive Model for Adaptive Learning Systems.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This paper proposes a theoretical framework for constructing a mathematical cognitive model intended to support cognitive diagnostic assessment and adaptive learning systems.

file_name: 128. Chen et al. (2020). Synergistic effects of instruction and affect factors on high‑ and low‑ability disparities in elementary students’ reading literacy.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PIRLS 2016 Grade 4 Reading data from English-instruction systems, the study leveraged reading self-concepts, reading attitudes, and selected teacher instructional practices (e.g., same-ability grouping and comprehension strategy instruction) to predict high vs low reading proficiency, finding that affective constructs—especially reading self-concepts—were the predominant and most influential factors for discriminating high from low achievers.

file_name: 129. Campos & Scherer. (2024). Digital gender gaps in Students’ knowledge, attitudes and skills: an integrative data analysis across 32 Countries.pdf
canonical_variable: Digital skills (CIL) via ICT affect mediation
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using ICILS 2013 & 2018 Grade 8 CIL the study leveraged Gender, ICT availability at home, and ICT affect to estimate direct and indirect effects on digital skills, finding that girls outperformed boys (negative direct effect) and that ICT affect partially mediated the gender gap (positive indirect effect).

file_name: 13. Bu & Chen. (2023). What key contextual factors contribute to students’ reading literacy among top-performing countries and economies? Statistical and machine learning analyses.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading the study leveraged assessing credibility, summarizing information, and ESCS (along with reading interest and understanding/remembering) to predict reading literacy (PV1READ), finding that metacognitive factors—especially assessing credibility—were the most influential predictors and that the GBDT model achieved R²≈0.50 with RMSE≈68.34 for the combined top-performing countries/economies.

file_name: 130. Chung et al. (2022). An International Comparison Study Exploring the Influential Variables Affecting Students’ Reading Literacy and Life Satisfaction.pdf
canonical_variable: Reading literacy (Plausible Values) | Life satisfaction (0–10 scale)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 reading literacy the study leveraged socioeconomic status (ESCS), grade repetition, metacognitive strategies, ICT interest, and long-text use in class to predict reading literacy, finding that ESCS and multiple metacognitive/self-concept variables were positively associated with reading literacy in both countries, while the effect of long-text use differed by country (positive in the U.S., negative in South Korea).

file_name: 131. Damiani et al. (2024). Young people’s civic engagement in Italy: evidence on grade 8 students from the international civic and citizenship education study (ICCS) 2016.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using ICCS 2016 Italy Grade 8 the study leveraged citizenship self-efficacy, trust in civic institutions, engagement with social media, openness in classroom discussions, and civic learning at school to predict civic engagement profile membership, finding that engaged students were characterized by higher citizenship self-efficacy (and generally higher levels of the other listed predictors), while civic knowledge showed no statistically significant association.

file_name: 14. Choi & Sung. (2024). Do Key Predictors of Academic Resilience Differ Across Cultures? Evidence From Korea and the US.pdf
canonical_variable: Academic resilience (binary: reading Level 3+ vs below) using 10 reading PVs
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for disadvantaged students (bottom-25% ESCS) in Korea and the US, the study leveraged meta-cognition variables (METASUM, METASPAM, UNDREM), reading self-concept (SCREADCOMP), and joy of reading (JOYREAD) to predict academic resilience (binary Level 3+ vs below) from reading plausible values, finding that individual cognitive/non-cognitive factors ranked highest in both countries while some predictors differed by culture (e.g., self-efficacy and educational expectation more prominent in Korea; fixed mindset and meaning in life more prominent in the US).

file_name: 15. Kalaycı-Alas & Demir. (2023). Heuristic analysis of the native language curriculum of  successful countries in pisa and turkey by using anfis.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading (country-level) together with native language curriculum document counts, the study leveraged Reading & listening outcomes and Reading & listening codes to predict the PISA 2018 reading score, finding a moderately strong and significant relationship (R²≈0.78) with RMSE≈20.57.

file_name: 16. Bernardo et al. (2021). Using Machine Learning Approaches to Explore Non-Cognitive Variables Influencing Reading Proficiency in English among Filipino Learners.pdf
canonical_variable: Reading proficiency group (low vs high) using PV1READ
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for the Philippines, the study leveraged perceived reading difficulty, exposure to bullying, mastery motivation/persistence, reading enjoyment, and socioeconomic status (ESCS) to classify students into low vs high reading proficiency groups (based on PV1READ), finding that the SVM achieved 81.20% test accuracy and AUC-ROC of 0.89; SHAP was used to identify the most influential variables, but the paper does not claim causal effects and does not report survey-weighted ML estimation.

file_name: 17. McJames et al. (2023). Factors affecting teacher job satisfaction  a causal inference machine learning approach using data from TALIS 2018.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TALIS 2018 (England) the study leveraged CPD participation, induction programme participation, and part-time contract status (along with other treatment indicators) to estimate causal effects on teacher job satisfaction, finding that CPD and induction had the strongest positive average treatment effects while part-time contracts had a negative effect; team teaching and observing showed positive directions but with wide uncertainty that included zero.

file_name: 18. Khine et al. (2025). From Meals to Marks: Modeling the Impact of Family Involvement on Reading Performance with Counterfactual Explainable AI.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 (UAE) reading plausible value the study leveraged family meal frequency, time talking with parents, and discussions about how school is going (plus other family-engagement items) to predict reading achievement, finding that Gradient Boosting achieved the best generalization (R² ≈ 0.188) and that counterfactual increases in SPENDTALK and EATMEAL could raise predicted reading scores by roughly 90 and 85 points respectively; however, the counterfactuals are univariate ceteris paribus simulations and SHAP-based importance is not causal evidence.

file_name: 19. Khoudi et al. (2024). Finding the contextual impacts on Students’ Mathematical performance using a Machine Learning-based Approach.pdf
canonical_variable: Low vs Others mathematics achievement (majority-vote benchmark)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2019 Grade 8 Mathematics data for Morocco, the study leveraged mathematics achievement too low, classroom libraries, science achievement too low, work on your own frequency, and student confidence in mathematics to predict low vs others mathematics achievement, finding that XGBoost achieved the best performance (Accuracy 81.92%, F1 74.42%) and identified 12 key contextual factors as most relevant.

file_name: 2. Zhu et al. (2025). Predictive insights into U.S. students’ mathematics performance on PISA 2022 using ensemble tree-based machine learning models.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: students, the study leveraged mathematics self-efficacy, ESCS, expected occupation status, familiarity with math concepts, and family support for self-directed learning to predict mathematics achievement (PVs), finding that these predictors were consistently among the most influential across ensemble tree models and that XGBoost provided the best accuracy–efficiency tradeoff.

file_name: 20. Bezek-Güre et al. (2020) Analysis of Factors Effecting PISA 2015 Mathematics Literacy via Educational Data Mining EN.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2015 Mathematics (Turkey) the study leveraged anxiety, Turkish success status, mother education level, motivation, and epistemological beliefs to predict students’ mathematics achievement status (low/medium/high).

file_name: 21. Koo & Yoo. (2025). Teachers’ team innovativeness in TALIS 2018: An empirical and simulation study using glmmLasso for multilevel data.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TALIS 2018 Korea the study leveraged teacher collaboration indicators (e.g., joint activities across classes/ages, common standards work, team conferences) and teacher self-efficacy to predict teachers’ team innovativeness (T3TEAM), finding that most key predictors had positive associations while the school climate item on shared responsibility for school issues (TT3G48D) showed a negative association; glmmLasso achieved comparable predictive error to LASSO/Enet but selected a substantially smaller, more stable set of predictors.

file_name: 22. Cheung et al. (2024). A machine-­ learning model of academic resilience in the times of the COVID-­ 19 pandemic: Evidence drawn from 79 countries:economies in the PISA 2022 mathematics study.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics the study leveraged math self-efficacy, math anxiety, familiarity with math concepts, truancy, and future aspirations to classify academic resilience status (ARS vs NRS), finding that the random-forest model achieved strong discrimination (ROC-AUC=96.79%) and that the top 35 features largely validated the prior resilience framework; SHAP was used to quantify feature contributions, but the paper is cross-sectional so SHAP-based importance should not be interpreted as causal effects.

file_name: 23. Encarnação et al. (2025). Hierarchical clustering of the pre- exam anxiety levels in physically inactive and active adolescent students from 56 countries: an observational study using PISA program data.pdf
canonical_variable: Pre-exam anxiety percentage (country-level) | Difference in pre-exam anxiety percentage (low vs high activity)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 the study leveraged physical activity status (low activity subgroup) to cluster countries by pre-exam anxiety percentage, finding that the most anxious countries formed two high-anxiety clusters (H-1: 67%, H-2: 78%) while three lower-anxiety clusters (L-1: 41%, L-2: 52%, L-3: 60%) emerged, with Brazil and the Dominican Republic among the highest-anxiety cluster members.

file_name: 24. Wang et al. (2022). Why do East Asian students do so well in mathematics? A machine learning study.pdf
canonical_variable: Mathematics achievement (Plausible Values; mean across five PV regressions)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2019 Grade 8 mathematics the study leveraged students’ confidence in mathematics, student SES, and school emphasis on academic success (among other socio-ecological factors) to predict mathematics achievement, finding that the top 11 predictors explained 49% of the variance (R²=0.49; RMSE=0.71) and that the three most important variables were students’ confidence, SES, and school academic emphasis.

file_name: 25. Anersen et al. (2025). Algorithmic Fairness in Automatic Short Answer Scoring.pdf
canonical_variable: Item correctness (correct vs incorrect) predicted from text | Automatic-vs-human scoring bias (demographic disparity) for gender and language groups
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2015 (Germany) reading-literacy item the study leveraged gender, language spoken at home, student performance level, psychometric item difficulty, and linguistic variance of responses to predict item correctness from short text responses, finding that the SVM with RoBERTa sentence embeddings achieved the highest overall agreement with human scoring (κ = 0.82) and that higher scoring accuracy generally corresponded to lower demographic disparity.

file_name: 26. Bencomo-Mesa et al. (2025). Inequality of Opportunity in Education in Spanish Regions: A Machine Learning Approach.pdf
canonical_variable: Inequality of opportunity in science achievement (IOpE)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 science data for Spain’s regions, the study leveraged number of books at home, household cultural possessions, and parental occupation (mother/father) to predict achievement within conditional inference trees/forests and then computed IOpE as the share of achievement variance explained by circumstances, finding that the preferred CIF specification yields IOpE more than twice the traditional linear-regression estimates (on average 43% vs 20%), with Murcia and Extremadura among the highest and Castilla-La Mancha and País Vasco among the lowest.

file_name: 27. Cai et al. (2025). A study on factors influencing digital sports participation among Chinese secondary school students based on explainable machine learning.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 (Hong Kong, China) the study leveraged academic performance, weekly PE days, household ICT resources, school ICT resources, and ICT social perception to predict students’ digital sports participation, finding that weekly PE days, household ICT resources, school ICT resources, and ICT social perception were positively associated with participation, while academic performance showed a negative association.

file_name: 28. Jung et al. (2024). Combining machine translation and automated scoring in international large-scale assessments.pdf
canonical_variable: Automated item scoring agreement (human vs machine) | Psychometric similarity of machine scores (AITC and item difficulty)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using TIMSS 2019 Grade 4 Science constructed-response the study leveraged machine translation engine choice, original response language, item identity, and country context to predict dichotomous correctness, finding that automated scoring with ANN classifiers achieved comparable human-machine agreement (average F1=0.88, kappa=0.80) with only marginal mean score differences (average SMD=-0.04), though performance was weaker for specific items/countries (notably Item 5 and Item 6 in C6).

file_name: 29. Huang et al. (2024). Applying Machine Learning and SHAP Method to Identify Key Influences on Middle-School Students’ Mathematics Literacy Performance.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy (PV1MATH) data from Hong Kong, Macao, Taipei, Singapore, Japan, and Korea, the study leveraged Math self-efficacy, expected occupational status, ESCS, weekend ICT leisure frequency, and math anxiety to predict mathematics literacy, finding that XGBoost achieved the best validation performance (R2=0.60; PCC=0.77) and that SHAP-based global explanations identified 15 key predictors with math self-efficacy as the strongest positive influence; the authors caution that SHAP explanations do not establish causal effects.

file_name: 3. Zhou et al. (2024). Investigating response behavior through TF-IDF and Word2vec text analysis_ A case study of PISA 2012 problem-solving process data.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2012 Problem Solving process data (Climate control CP02501) the study leveraged VOTAT navigation behavior, sequence length, reset frequency, and 0_0_0 behavior usage to predict correct answer (binary 0/1), finding that Random Forest with TF-IDF features achieved the best overall performance (F1=0.8419; Accuracy=0.8231; Recall=0.8572).

file_name: 30. Wang et al. (2023). Understanding Students’ Subjective and Eudaimonic Well‑Being: Combining both Machine Learning and Classical Statistics.pdf
canonical_variable: Positive affect (SWBP) | Negative affect (SWBN) | Life satisfaction (SWBL) | Eudaimonic well-being (EUDA; meaning in life)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 Hong Kong the study leveraged sense of belonging to school and resilience (plus additional microsystem/mesosystem factors) to predict positive affect, finding that school belonging was the most salient predictor and resilience was the second most influential factor (best model achieved R²=19.15% with RMSE=0.81 under 10-fold CV).

file_name: 31. Zhang & Cutumisu. (2024). Predicting the Mathematics Literacy of Resilient Student.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 Mathematics literacy data for resilient students in Eastern cultures, the study leveraged mathematics self-efficacy (21st-century and formal/applied), mathematics anxiety, effort/persistence, and working-in-household indicators to predict mathematics literacy (PVs 1–10) with Random Forest and Lasso; Random Forest achieved lower RMSE/MAE than Lasso and the most important predictors in Eastern cultures included MATHEF21, MATHEFF, and ANXMAT.

file_name: 32. Resinoet al. (2024). Student well-being and mathematical literacy performance in PISA 2018  a machine-learning approach.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 (Spain) mathematical literacy plausible values the study leveraged social well-being variables (talking to parents’ partners and time with friends) together with physical and material well-being indicators to predict mathematical literacy, finding that social well-being—especially communication with parents’ partners and peer time—was among the most influential predictors and that the best-performing models were selected via lowest RMSE across plausible values.

file_name: 84. Gong et al. (2023). Using process features to investigate scientific problem-solving in large-scale assessments.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using NAEP 2018 Science pilot fair test 1 (Grade 8) the study leveraged preparation time (PT), execution time (ET), and mean execution time (MET) to analyze rubric-based item scores, finding that higher-scoring students tended to spend less time executing actions and (for full-score vs lowest-score) less time preparing and executing on average.

file_name: 85. Ludwig et al. (2024). Predicting problem-solving success in an office simulation applying N-grams and a random forest to behavioral process data.pdf
canonical_variable: Problem-solving success (binary class; more vs less successful)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using Luca office-simulation log data from VET students, the study leveraged early-window process indicators captured via n-gram/bigram behavior patterns—especially note-taking, relevant document access, spreadsheet calculation, and attachment-download sequences—to predict binary problem-solving success, finding that predictive performance improves as the early window expands from 5 to 20 minutes (AUC ROC 0.61→0.67) and further improves with feature selection (up to ~0.70).

policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2012 computer-based assessment the study leveraged student math attitudes, books at home, mathematics efficacy, and out-of-school study time variables to predict three math proficiency classes, finding that training with synthetic data (especially CTGAN for minority-class recall and G-mean) improved minority-class prediction while majority-class performance deteriorated.

file_name: 87. Kara. (2025). Exploring Test-Taking Disengagement in the Context of PISA 2022: Evidence from Process Data.pdf
canonical_variable: Engagement profile membership (LPA profiles) | Mathematics achievement (profile differences)
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2022 (Turkey) mathematics the study leveraged response time (RT), number of actions (NA), and self-reported effort (SRE) to classify students into engagement profiles, finding three distinct groups: Moderately Engaged (Profile 1, 82.79%), Highly Engaged (Profile 2, 7.20%), and Disengaged (Profile 3, 10.01%).

file_name: 88. Park et al. (2023). Understanding students’ problem-solving patterns: Evidence from an allotted response time in a PISA 2012 item.pdf
policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2012 CPS Ticket item CP038q1 data for students with the correct answer, the study leveraged time_start, time_b1, time_b3, time_irrelevant, and time_avg_btw_events to predict overall problem-solving ability (PISA scale-score ratio), finding that these time-related process variables were the most consistently selected and yielded a test-set RMSE of 0.18.

policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA 2018 data for OECD countries, the study leveraged total weekly teaching load (CPERWEEK), foreign-language weekly lessons (CPERFORLAN), ICT resources (ICTRES), ESCS, and highest parental occupational status (HISEI) to predict country success in reading, finding that CPERWEEK and CPERFORLAN were among the strongest predictors while metacognition credibility (METASPAM) had the lowest coefficient among the selected set.

policy_recommendation_excerpt: Using PISA the study leveraged the reported predictors to predict Literature synthesis outcome (not student-level prediction), finding that This is not an empirical ML study; it is a methodological paper arguing for design-based inference as the preferred basis for official reporting in international large-scale assessments.

canonical_variable: Mathematics ability (latent ability) | Test-taking speed (latent speed)
